coredata.dc:title: L-cysteine derived Golgi apparatus-targeting carbon dots for visualizing glutathione fluctuations during ferroptosis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Mengyao
Huang, Yongfei
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Ferroptosis is a form of cell death induced by iron accumulation of lipid peroxides. The Golgi apparatus maintains intracellular redox homeostasis during ferroptosis by regulating glutathione (GSH). Therefore, real-time monitoring of GSH levels on the Golgi apparatus is essential to comprehend its function in the progress of ferroptosis and investigate the mechanisms of ferroptosis-mediated diseases. Herein, we synthesized carbon dots, L-Cys-CDs, with excellent photostability under a low-temperature oil bath at 80 °C employing L-cysteine as carbon source. L-Cys-CDs exhibited fast response and excellent selectivity to GSH. The investigations on the targeting mechanism indicated that the L-type stereo-structure and free sulfhydryl groups synergistically resulted in the ability of L-Cys-CDs to target the Golgi apparatus specifically. Due to the overexpression of GSH in cancer cells, L-Cys-CDs allowed differentiation of normal cells and cancer cells. More importantly, L-Cys-CDs could be applied to monitor the reduction of GSH during ferroptosis in cells and zebrafish, and its reversal by ferroptosis inhibitors. In light of these properties, L-Cys-CDs will provide an opportunity for a more in-depth insight into the role of GSH in ferroptosis, which is important for research into the pathological pathway and therapeutic choice for ferroptosis-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Golgi apparatus
Glutathione
Cell recognition
Ferroptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Golgi apparatus oxidative stress response

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image); (B) Size distribution; (C) XRD pattern of L-Cys-CDs; (D) FT-IR spectra of L-Cys/L-Cys-CDs; (E) XPS spectrum of L-Cys-CDs. High-resolution XPS of (F) C 1s; (G) N 1s; (H) O 1s and (I) S 2p of L-Cys-CDs.

(A) UV–vis, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of L-Cys-CDs (Inset: Pictures of L-Cys-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light); (B) Emission spectrum of L-Cys-CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (C) CD spectrum of L-Cys/L-Cys-CDs. Emission spectrum of L-Cys-CDs in solutions with (D) different ratios of 1,4-dioxane to water; (E) different ratios of glycerol to water; (F) different pH.

(A) Fluorescence spectra variation of L-Cys-CDs to GSH; (B) Linear fitting of ratiometric Fluorescence intensity (F0/F) and glutathione concentration (0–800 μM); (C) Time-course of probe L-Cys-CDs in the presence of GSH; (D) Selectivity of the L-Cys-CDs for GSH over ions (1 mM), amino acids (1 mM), RSS (200 μM), RNS (200 μM), and ROS (200 μM); (E) Interference of the L-Cys-CDs after adding GSH and ions (1 mM), amino acids (1 mM), RSS (200 μM), RNS (200 μM), and ROS (200 μM); (F) Fluorescence lifetimes of L-Cys-CDs with various levels of GSH.

Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells incubated with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL), and then stained with 1 μM GA-tracker (a1-a3), 1 μM Mito-Tracker (b1-b3), 1 μM ER-Tracker (c1-c3), 1 μM Lyso-Tracker (d1-d3); Pearson’s correlation coefficient between L-Cys-CDs and different organelle dyes (a4, b4, c4, d4). Scale bar: 25 μm.

Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells treated with L-Cys-CDs(a1-a3), D-Cys-CDs (b1-b3), L-Trp-CDs (c1-c3), TAA-CDs (d1-d3), and stained with 1 μM GA-tracker for 20 min; Pearson’s correlation coefficient between CDs prepared using different carbon sources and GA-tracker (a4, b4, c4, d4). Scale bar: 25 μm.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of cancer HeLa (a1-a3), HepG 2 Cells (b1-b3) and PC-12 cells (c1-c3), and normal HL-7702 (d1-d3), HUVEC (e1-e3) and MC3T3-E1 cells (f1-f3), all of which were treated with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL). (B) Mean fluorescence intensity of the corresponding images. Scale bar: 25 μm.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells stained without (blank, a1-a3) and with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL) (control, b1-b3); HeLa cells treated with 10 μM erastin for 4 h (c1-c3), 8 h (d1-d3), and 12 h (e1-e3), or co-incubated with 10 μM erastin and 15 μM Fer-1 for 12 h, and then stained with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL) for 20 min (f1-f3). (B) Mean fluorescence intensity of the corresponding images. (C) Consumption of cellular GSH during ferroptosis. Scale bar: 25 μm.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells stained without (blank, a1-a3) and with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL) (control, b1-b3); HeLa cells treated with 10 μM erastin for 12 h (c1-c3); co-incubated with 10 μM erastin and 100 μM DFO (d1-d3), 20 μM VE (e1-e3), or 50 μM rutin for 12 h (f1-f3) and subsequently stained with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL) for 20 min. (B) Mean fluorescence intensity of the corresponding images. Scale bar: 25 μm.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of zebrafish stained without (blank, a1-a3) and with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL) (control, b1-b3); zebrafish treated with 10 μM erastin for 12 h (c1-c3); zebrafish co-incubated with 10 μM erastin and 15 μM Fer-1 (d1-d2), 100 μM DFO (e1-e3), 20 μM VE (f1-f3) or 50 μM rutin (g1-g3) for 12 h, subsequently stained with L-Cys-CDs (250 μg/mL) for another 20 min. (B) Mean fluorescence intensity of the corresponding images. Scale bar: 200 μm.
Preparation of L-Cys-CDs and its application for real-time monitoring of the GSH in Golgi apparatus during ferroptosis.

head.ce:title: L-cysteine derived Golgi apparatus-targeting carbon dots for visualizing glutathione fluctuations during ferroptosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030031, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferroptosis is a form of cell death induced by iron accumulation of lipid peroxides. The Golgi apparatus maintains intracellular redox homeostasis during ferroptosis by regulating glutathione (GSH). Therefore, real-time monitoring of GSH levels on the Golgi apparatus is essential to comprehend its function in the progress of ferroptosis and investigate the mechanisms of ferroptosis-mediated diseases. Herein, we synthesized carbon dots, L-Cys-CDs, with excellent photostability under a low-temperature oil bath at 80 °C employing L-cysteine as carbon source. L-Cys-CDs exhibited fast response and excellent selectivity to GSH. The investigations on the targeting mechanism indicated that the L-type stereo-structure and free sulfhydryl groups synergistically resulted in the ability of L-Cys-CDs to target the Golgi apparatus specifically. Due to the overexpression of GSH in cancer cells, L-Cys-CDs allowed differentiation of normal cells and cancer cells. More importantly, L-Cys-CDs could be applied to monitor the reduction of GSH during ferroptosis in cells and zebrafish, and its reversal by ferroptosis inhibitors. In light of these properties, L-Cys-CDs will provide an opportunity for a more in-depth insight into the role of GSH in ferroptosis, which is important for research into the pathological pathway and therapeutic choice for ferroptosis-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In contrast to prokaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells have subcellular organelles, including mitochondria, lysosomes, lipid droplets, Golgi apparatus, endoplasmic reticulum, and nucleolus [1]. Different organelles perform different tasks due to its distinct compositions while responsible for cellular growth, metabolism, and apoptosis [2]. As a major organelle, the Golgi apparatus is essential to the diversity of metabolic pathways and cell signaling since its main biological functions include the collection, processing, and sorting of protein [3]. It has been established that Golgi apparatus stress, which is brought on by disruptions in the structure and function of the Golgi apparatus, alters redox homeostasis and affects cell survival, leading to various illnesses like inflammation, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson’s disease, and cancer [4–8]. Therefore, a comprehensive overview of the physiological functions and pathological processes associated with the Golgi apparatus requires in situ real-time monitoring of bioactives in the Golgi apparatus. Classical methods such as silver staining, electron microscope imaging, and ceramide derivative staining have been shown to image the intracellular Golgi apparatus, whereas they do not apply to the real-time monitoring of bioactives in the Golgi apparatus [9]. Moreover, the field concerning Golgi-targeted probes remains in its nascent stages in comparison with other significant organelles like mitochondria and lysosomes.

Glutathione (GSH), which is the most plentiful thiol in the cell, is directly involved in neutralizing reactive oxygen species and free radicals to maintain the immune system's balance within the cell, and its concentration level is an important indicator of the normalcy of biological systems [10]. Ferroptosis, as proposed by Stockwell et al. in 2012, is a distinct iron-dependent, non-apoptotic mode of cellular death associated with iron accumulation, lipid peroxidation, and reduced glutathione depletion [11,12]. In the last decade, the exponential growth of studies on ferroptosis has been attributed to the fact that the development of diseases such as cardiovascular disease, liver injury, Alzheimer's disease, and cancer is strongly associated with ferroptosis [13,14]. Pharmacological Golgi stress inducers (e.g., brefeldin A and golgicide A) trigger ferroptosis, and this can be avoided by overexpression of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), indicating that Golgi-dependent ferroptosis requires classical ferroptotic regulators [15,16]. As a crucial organelle, the Golgi apparatus is involved in lipid synthesis and processing, and can influence intracellular redox homeostasis by participating in the regulation of GSH secretion, which leads to the enhancement or inhibition of ferroptosis [17,18]. Therefore, there is a tremendous need for real-time tracking and monitoring of changes in glutathione levels on the Golgi apparatus during ferroptosis, to unravel the mechanism of action of ferroptosis, to the point of understanding and diagnosing diseases associated with ferroptosis.

Fluorescent probes have emerged in recent years as a potent method to measure bioactives at the subcellular organelle level owing to their superior sensitivity and selectivity, real-time monitoring, non-destructive detection, and excellent spatiotemporal resolution [19–21]. Compared with common fluorescent probes, such as organic small molecule probes, semiconductor quantum dots, and noble metal nanoclusters, carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing focus due to lower cost, excellent photostability, very low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility [22–25]. Previous methods for detecting GSH based on CDs started with the introduction of metal ions or other analytes that bonded to the CDs causing a fluorescence quenching. After the addition of GSH, the analyte leaves the CDs, and its fluorescence was recovered [26–28]. These detection methods were complicated and the introduction of metal ions caused toxicity, making it unsuitable for applications in living organisms. Chiral carbon dots (CCDs) not only inherit the advantages of traditional CDs, but also expand its applications in areas including chiral recognition, antimicrobial, and selective modulation of cellular energy metabolism because of unique chiral signals [29,30]. CCDs have been applied to the targeting of the Golgi apparatus. Yuan et al. synthesized blue-emitting CCDs L-Pen-CDs and D-Pen-CDs by a two-step approach employing citric acid and L-/D-penicillamine as carbon and chiral source, and both two CCDs exhibited Golgi-targeting properties with Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.71 and 0.84, respectively [31]. Li et al. synthesized CCDs with abundant cysteine residues and steric structures by a two-step pyrolysis method employing citric acid and L-/D-cysteine as the carbon and chiral sources, of which LC-CQD showed excellent long-term Golgi apparatus targeting (Pearson correlation coefficient higher than 0.9) and imaging capabilities [32]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there have been no reports of CCDs real-time detecting relevant bioactives in the Golgi apparatus in situ.

To address the above problems, we synthesized L-Cys-CDs by reflux heating utilizing L-cysteine (L-Cys) as carbon source, the mild experimental conditions resulted in high preservation of the chiral stereo structure and free sulfhydryl groups of the precursor. L-Cys-CDs could detect GSH specifically within 1 min and was unaffected by polarity, viscosity, pH, and complex biological environment. The linear range was 0–800 μM (LOD = 0.29 μM). The investigations on the targeting mechanism indicated that the L-type stereo-structure and free sulfhydryl groups synergistically resulted in the ability of L-Cys-CDs to target the Golgi apparatus specifically. Meanwhile, L-Cys-CDs would serve as an effective tool to distinguish normal cells and tumor cells. Fluorescence confocal experiments showed that L-Cys-CDs exhibited excellent photostability and was applied to monitor the change in GSH under Golgi stress by monensin-stimulated. Furthermore, L-Cys-CDs demonstrated the dynamic evolution of GSH levels on the Golgi apparatus during ferroptosis, which might provide a viable option for the research of ferroptosis mechanisms and the treatment of its related diseases (Scheme 1 ).
The supporting information included the details of the materials and equipment needed for the experiment.

L-Cys-CDs was synthesized as follows: 300 mg of L-Cys and 80 mg NaOH added into 20 mL of deionized water, the mixture was put into a round-bottomed flask and allowed to react at 80 °C for 24 h. The fluid was dialyzed with 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. Subsequently, L-Cys-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying.
D-Cys-CDs, L-Trp-CDs, and TAA-CDs were synthesized using the same method mentioned above except that the corresponding amino acids were used instead of L-Cys.

L-Cys-CDs was dissolved in deionized to obtain a 10 mg/mL solution. 50 µL of a 10 mg/mL L-Cys-CDs solution was combined with 10 μL of PBS buffer (0.2 M, pH = 7.4), a variety of GSH concentrations were added, the mixture was diluted to 2 mL with PBS buffer, and then incubated for one minute. Each experiment was carried out three times, recording fluorescence spectra from 420 nm to 700 nm under an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

Various technological methods were employed to characterize the morphology and elemental composition of L-Cys-CDs. L-Cys-CDs was uniformly quasi-spherical with a size range of 2.04–4.25 nm with a mean particle size of 3.11 nm (Fig. 1 A and 1B). High-resolution TEM demonstrated that L-Cys-CDs with a lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm, matched the interplanar spacing of graphene (100 faces) [33]. The XRD pattern displayed a strong and broad diffraction peak of L-Cys-CDs at 22.21°, which corresponded to the (002) plane of the graphite structure (Fig. 1C), suggesting that the highly conjugated sp2 structure accumulated the CDs [34]. The functional groups on the surface of L-Cys-CDs were examined by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 3200–3600 cm−1 should be assigned to the O-H/N-H stretching vibration, and the peak at 2975 cm−1 was caused by the C-H stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 2575 cm−1 was correlated with S-H, the signals generated at 1615 cm−1 and 1492 cm−1 were for C=O and C=C/C=N stretching vibrations, respectively, and the C-S stretching vibration was located at 675 cm−1. The above data indicated that functional groups including amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and sulfhydryl were present on the surface of L-Cys-CDs.

Elements components and structure of L-Cys-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). L-Cys-CDs showed four peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.1 eV, 531.1 eV, and 164.1 eV that were attributed to C (45.37 %), N (10.23 %), O (31.11 %), and S (13.29 %), respectively (Fig. 1E). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum allowed deconvolution into three peaks at 287.9 eV (C=O), 285.6 eV (C-O/C-O/C-S), and 284.6 eV (C-C, sp2 C), with the maximum content of sp2 C, indicating a higher degree of graphitization of L-Cys-CDs (Fig. 1F, Table S1). The deconvolution spectrum of N 1s could observe three peaks attributed to 401.0 eV (graphitic N), 399.4 eV (pyrrole N,) and 398.8 eV (pyridine N) (Fig. 1G). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum could be deconvolved into two peaks of 532.8 eV (C=O) and 531.5 eV (C-O) (Fig. 1H). The high-resolution S 2p spectrum was fitted to two peaks of 164.2 eV and 163.0 eV, which corresponded to spin–orbit coupled S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. 1I). In summary, L-Cys-CDs was successfully synthesized with homogeneous size and numerous groups on the surface, providing a favorable foundation for broadening its applications.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the two UV absorption peaks that L-Cys-CDs exhibited were at 204 nm and 300 nm. These peaks matched the π-π* and n-π* transitions of C=C and C-O/C-N bond. Furthermore, the best excitation wavelength of L-Cys-CDs was 405 nm, while the maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at 516 nm. The emission spectra of L-Cys-CDs under various excitation wavelengths were measured (Fig. 2B). The maximum emission wavelength of L-Cys-CDs was constant at 516 nm when the excitation wavelength was raised from 350 nm to 450 nm, suggesting that L-Cys-CDs had luminescence properties independent of the excitation wavelength. TEM and XPS indicated that L-Cys-CDs displayed evenly crystalline core and highly graphitized, which might contribute to the excitation wavelength independence of L-Cys-CDs [35]. The chiral properties of the precursor L-Cys and its product L-Cys-CDs were analyzed by circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy (Fig. 2C). L-Cys had a significant chiral signal at 203 nm. At a synthesis temperature that was significantly lower than the precursor melting point (220 °C), the chiral signal of L-Cys-CDs at 198 nm was inherited from L-Cys with a minor blueshift, which might be owing to the π-π stacking that resulted in the substitution of surface groups by electron-withdrawing group. Furthermore, the new symmetric chiral signals of CCDs at 218 nm and 250 nm might be caused by the asymmetric electric field arising from the chiral group breaking the symmetry of the electronic states in the achiral cores [36].

In the progression of Golgi-related diseases, microenvironmental parameters (pH, polarity, viscosity, etc.) in the Golgi apparatus were also altered, which interfered with its signaling pathways [37]. As the 1,4-dioxane concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength remained essentially constant (Fig. 2D). Then, the fluorescence emission spectra of L-Cys-CDs was measured in solutions of glycerol and water mixed in different ratios (Fig. 2E). The data demonstrated that the emission spectrum of L-Cys-CDs did not change markedly when the polarity and viscosity of the solution was varied. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of L-Cys-CDs did not change significantly over the physiological pH range (Fig. 2F). The optical stability of L-Cys-CDs was measured in various environments. The luminescence intensity was 93.5 % of the initial value after 120 min of exposure to 365 nm UV light, indicating that possessed excellent anti-photobleaching property (Fig. S1A). The luminescence intensity of L-Cys-CDs was almost constant when incubated at 0 °C to 80 °C, as well as the fluorescence intensity was maintained over 60 days at 4 °C, suggesting that L-Cys-CDs had excellent temperature stability and storage stability(Fig. S1B and S1C). The fluorescence signal was maintained at NaCl concentrations of 0–1 mol/L, showing that analytical determination was not interfered with in conventional ionic strength solutions(Fig. S1D). It could be concluded that L-Cys-CDs possess excellent photostability under a wide range of complex conditions and could be employed to identify analytes in complex samples.

Firstly, the effect of altered pH upon the reaction of L-Cys-CDs to GSH was investigated. L-Cys-CDs responded well to GSH when the pH was from 4.0 to 8.0, so physiological condition (pH = 7.4) was chosen for subsequent experiments (Fig. S2). The optical response of L-Cys-CDs to GSH was examined under optimal conditions. With increasing GSH levels, the luminescence intensity of L-Cys-CDs declined continuously while the emission peak position did not change, suggesting that the band structure of L-Cys-CDs remains unchanged (Fig. 3 A) [38]. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) displayed linear relationships with GSH in the range of 0–800 μM (Fig. 3B). The linear equation was y = 0.00245 x + 0.870 (R2 = 0.992), and the LOD was 0.29 µM. L-Cys-Cds could directly detect GSH with a wider detection range and lower LOD than other CDs-based fluorescent sensors (Table S2). The fluorescence intensity of the system achieved equilibrium within 1 min after the addition of GSH and remained constant after that (Fig. 3C).

Subsequently, the optical response of L-Cys-CDs to other related substances, such as ions, amino acids, reactive sulfur species (RSS), reactive nitrogen species (RNS), and reactive oxygen species (ROS) was investigated to demonstrate the superior selectivity of L-Cys-CDs for GSH. In contrast to the addition of GSH, which caused a sharp increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F, other substances did not change the value of F0/F (Fig. 3D). Other substances were added to the GSH-quenched solution of L-Cys-CDs, which suggested that the effect of other interfering substances was negligible (Fig. 3E). The luminescence intensity of the GSH-burst L-Cys-CDs solution was almost invariant in solutions of different viscosity and polarity, suggesting that the system is insensitive to viscosity and polarity (Fig. S3). That meant that the L-Cys-CDs exhibited high selectivity for GSH without interference from related substances in the physiological environment, indicating that it could be applied to accurately measure the change of GSH in biosystems.

Then, the mechanism of the response of L-Cys-CDs to GSH was examined. Since the absorption peak of GSH at 210 nm did not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of L-Cys-CDs, there was no internal filtering effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. S4). The absorption spectra of L-Cys-CDs did not change by GSH addition, indicating that no state complex was formed and the static quenching mechanism was excluded. Fluorescence lifetime of L-Cys-CDs decreased to 2.84 ns from 6.27 ns with the addition of GSH (Fig. 3F, Table S3). Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer constants (KSV) of the L-Cys-CD and GSH systems increased with rising reaction temperature (Fig. S5). Hence, it was inferred that the interaction of GSH with excited L-Cys-CDs led to fluorescence quenching, which was dynamic quenching.

The cytotoxicity of L-Cys-CDs was negligible over a wide concentration range and different incubation times (Fig. S6). Moreover, L-Cys-CDs still maintained excellent anti-photobleaching and photostability in cells (Fig. S7 and S8), indicating its potential capability to be applied for cellular imaging. L-Cys residues had often been employed as Golgi-targeting groups [39–41]. Therefore, the ability of L-Cys-CDs to specifically target the Golgi apparatus was investigated (Fig. 4 ). HeLa cells were stained with a Golgi targeting dye (GA-tracker) after incubation with L-Cys-CDs. It demonstrated that L-Cys-CDs highly overlap with GA-tracker and the Pearson correlation coefficient was 0.97. In contrast, L-Cys-CDs overlapped poorly with mitochondrial dye (Mito-tracker), endoplasmic reticulum dye (ER-tracker), and lysosomal dye (Lyso-tracker), and the Pearson correlation coefficient was 0.38, 0.47, and 0.41, respectively, indicating the superior Golgi-targeting capability of L-Cys-CDs.

Subsequently, the mechanism of L-Cys-CDs targeting to the Golgi apparatus was investigated. D-Cys-CDs, L-Trp-CDs, and TAA-CDs were synthesized under identical conditions using D-cysteine (D-Cys), L-tryptophan (L-Trp), and thioacetamide (TAA) as precursors (Fig. S9, S10, and S11). The only distinguishing between D-Cys-CDs and L-Cys-CDs was the different chiral signaling which caused by the steric structure of the precursor. D-Cys-CDs displayed limited Golgi-targeting ability with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.66 (Fig. 5 ). Measurement of the content of free sulfhydryl groups of L-/D-Cys-CDs demonstrated that the sulfhydryl group content was essentially equal (Fig. S12), so that the variation in the ability to target the Golgi apparatus was due only to differences in steric structure. L-Trp had the same stereo structure as L-Cys but no sulfhydryl group. L-Trp-CDs was randomly distributed in the cytoplasm and had no specificity for the Golgi apparatus. TAA had neither a free sulfhydryl group nor chiral stereo structure, and TAA-CDs exhibited no Golgi-targeting ability with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.42. Iodoacetamide (IAM) was frequently employed as a sulfhydryl group sealer because it alkylates the sulfhydryl group's sulfur atom [42]. Both FT-IR spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectra demonstrated that L-Cys-CDs treated by IAM didn't show S-H related absorption peaks (Fig. S13) and that the sulfhydryl group closure didn't cause significant changes in the optical properties of L-Cys-CDs. More importantly, unlike L-Cys-CDs, IAM-treated L-Cys-CDs were randomly distributed in the cytoplasm and had a less effective targeting of the Golgi apparatus, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.45. The results further confirmed that free sulfhydryl groups and appropriate steric structure synergized to provide L-Cys-CDs with excellent Golgi-targeting ability.

Competitive inhibition experiments were subsequently performed (Fig. S13). Cells were incubated with L-Cys, D-Cys, L-Trp, and TAA followed by L-Cys-CDs and GA-tracker. The overlap between L-Cys-CDs and GA-tracker was weak and the Pearson correlation coefficient decreased to 0.58 after L-Cys addition, since L-Cys preferentially binds to sites on the Golgi apparatus, inhibiting the targeting ability of L-Cys-CDs [32]. The ability of D-Cys to bind to the Golgi apparatus was relatively low, and it was poorly able to inhibit the targeting of L-Cys-CDs. L-Trp and TAA did not compete with L-Cys-CDs for binding to the Golgi apparatus and therefore could not inhibit the targeting ability of L-Cys-CDs. It has been reported that both galactosyltransferase and protein kinase D are able to anchor to the Golgi apparatus using Cys residues or Cys-rich domains [43,44]. Through space structure assistance, the sulfhydryl group on the L-Cys residue could be anchored to the Golgi apparatus by generating a disulfide bond in the oxidative environment within the Golgi apparatus lumen and binding to its receptor.

The ability of L-Cys-CDs to detect GSH in living cells was further investigated (Fig. S14). HeLa cells that were incubated with L-Cys-CDs showed green fluorescence. N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) was a common thiol scavenger. Fluorescence intensity was higher in cells treated with NEM than in the control group without NEM due to the reduction of GSH content in the cells. After adding different concentrations of GSH, the fluorescence intensity in the cells gradually decreased. Consequently, L-Cys-CDs allowed the detection of endogenous and exogenous GSH at the cellular level.

It has been shown that GSH is overexpressed in tumor cells, therefore a significant difference in GSH levels exists between normal and tumor cells. The potential of L-Cys-CDs to distinguish cancer cells and normal cells was evaluated. A variety of cancer cells (HeLa, HepG2, PC-12) and normal cells (HL-7702, HUVEC, MC3T3-E1) were selected for the assay. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , only a weak fluorescent signal was detected when cancer cells were incubated with L-Cys-CDs. In contrast, when normal cells were treated with L-Cys-CDs under the same conditions, it displayed significant fluorescence, which was about 2-fold stronger than cancerous cells. The above results indicated that L-Cys-CDs was suitable for distinguishing cancer cells and normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Golgi apparatus oxidative stress response
ce:section.ce:para: It was well known that oxidative stress had been characterized by the generation of ROS and exhaustion of antioxidants, thus GSH took an essential position in the Golgi apparatus oxidative stress process. Monensin (Mone), a Golgi apparatus stress inducer, upregulated the expression of cystathionine γ-lyase (CSE) leading to the increase of GSH in the Golgi apparatus [45]. Therefore, a cellular model of Mone-induced oxidative stress was established (Fig. S15). HeLa cells exhibited green fluorescence after incubation with L-Cys-CDs. After stimulation of HeLa cells with Mone and then incubation with L-Cys-CDs, the intracellular fluorescence intensity decreased continuously with the increase of the stimulation time, which might be due to the increase of intracellular GSH under the stimulation of Mone. This meant that L-Cys-CDs could detect fluctuations of the GSH content in the Golgi apparatus in situ during Mone-induced oxidative stress.

It had been known that erastin inhibited the ingestion of cysteine, which exhausted GSH in the cells, eventually leading to ferroptosis [46]. Cells were stimulated with erastin and then incubated with L-Cys-CDs. The fluorescence intensity in erastin-stimulated cells continuously increased with the duration of stimulation compared to the control group which was without erastin, suggesting that the GSH concentration was decreasing during ferroptosis. Ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1) was a putative ferroptosis inhibitor by preventing the excessive accumulation of lipid peroxides [47]. Co-incubation of cells with Fer-1 and erastin, followed by staining with L-Cys-CDs, it was showed that the fluorescence intensity was essentially the same as the control group, demonstrating that Fer-1 inhibited the depletion of GSH in the Golgi apparatus during ferroptosis (Fig. 7 ).

Subsequently, we investigated the effects of deferoxamine (DFO), vitamin E (VE), and rutin on glutathione levels in the Golgi apparatus during ferroptosis (Fig. 8 ). The mechanism of inhibition of ferroptosis by DFO and VE was based on the chelation of Fe(II) and antioxidant activity, respectively, while rutin exhibited both of these effects [48]. Similarly, cells were co-incubated with erastin and DFO, VE, and rutin, respectively, and followed by staining with L-Cys-CDs. The cells displayed green fluorescence with similar intensity as the control group. The above results confirmed that L-Cys-CDs was a promising candidate for real-time monitoring of the fluctuation of GSH levels during ferroptosis and could potentially be used for evaluating the effects of drugs produced in the process of ferroptosis.

Taking into account the excellent performance of L-Cys-CDs in successfully responding to GSH fluctuations during ferroptosis in living cells, we attempted to detect GSH during ferroptosis in vivo. Among living vertebrates, zebrafish were particularly suitable for in vivo imaging owing to its high similarity to the human genome and optical translucency [49,50]. As shown in Fig. 9 , zebrafish pretreated with erastin and then incubated with L-Cys-CDs exhibited stronger green fluorescence than zebrafish incubated with L-Cys-CDs only. Zebrafish displayed similar fluorescence properties to the control group when co-incubated with erastin and the ferroptosis inhibitor Fer-1/DFO/VE/Rutin. Therefore, L-Cys-CDs could observe the ferroptosis process as well as inhibition of ferroptosis followed by alteration of GSH in zebrafish. Taken together, L-Cys-CDs for the detection of GSH fluctuations could be an effective tool to visualize the microdynamics in ferroptosis.

In conclusion, L-Cys-CDs was synthesized by a one-step reflux heating method for GSH real-time monitoring in the ferroptosis model. The L-Cys-CDs exhibited excellent optical stability, fast response to GSH specificity, and excellent bioimaging performance. Under the synergistic effect of the L-type steric structure and free sulfhydryl groups, L-Cys-CDs displayed outstanding Golgi apparatus targeting ability. With the detection of endogenous GSH levels, L-Cys-CDs could differentiate normal cells and cancer cells. More importantly, the downregulation of GSH levels was observed by L-Cys-CDs during ferroptosis in HeLa cells and zebrafish, and its reversal by ferroptosis inhibitors. Nevertheless, the luminescence wavelength of L-Cys-CDs was located in the green light region, which limited its application in vivo. It confirmed that L-Cys-CDs could be applied as an efficient tool to detect the fluctuation level of GSH in the Golgi apparatus in situ, and also facilitate the understanding of the redox state of the Golgi apparatus during ferroptosis. That could provide insight into the mechanisms associated with oxidative stress and ferroptosis-related disease models.

Mengyao Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yongfei Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Zhefeng Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116566.

ce:section.ce:para:
